next: requires manual or scene-text grounding before a quantitative comparison

measurement_attempt.goal: Measure whether prompt strategies move mark stroke weight toward the brand's.
measurement_attempt.outcome: ABANDONED — not measurable with an automated locator.
measurement_attempt.what_failed: Each generation places the shoe differently, so the mark must be found per image. The automated locator defined a wordmark as 'a row of small, similar-sized, roughly collinear bright components'. That is also an exact description of a line of STITCHING. It locked onto the heel-collar seam in all four images, reporting 14-25 'letters' for a 7-letter mark, and produced stroke-width ratios (1.28 / 0.80 / 1.01 / 1.25) that straddle 1.0 and would have read as 'one prompt matched the brand exactly'.
measurement_attempt.why_it_is_reported: The numbers were discarded, not published. They are recorded here because the failure is evidence for the report's main claim (section 7): grounding is the hard part. A generic detector cannot tell a wordmark from a seam, which is the same reason a hand-declared box did not correspond to the pocket in Task 4.
measurement_attempt.what_would_be_needed: Text-specific detection (a scene-text detector or SAM-class segmentation), or hand-declared boxes per generation. Not attempted; out of budget for this pass.
measurement_attempt.what_survives: The n=2 result stands on direct inspection at 8x (logo_zoom.png): a plain prompt and a prompt given the mark as a reference image PLUS explicit anti-restyling constraints both re-drew the mark in the wrong typeface. Two further strategies (attributes, negative constraints) were generated and are on disk; they were not measured.

files_on_disk: ceiling_s2_attributes.png and ceiling_s3_negatives.png only. s1/s4 were byte-identical copies of p1_plain.png / p2_constrained.png and were removed to avoid implying four independent generations.
